name: Birria Tacos
desc: Crispy corn tortillas dipped in consomé and filled with tender braised beef birria and melted cheese.
steps: Toast and rehydrate chiles, blend with onion, garlic, and spices into a smooth sauce.
Braise beef in the chile sauce with broth for 2 hours until fork-tender, then shred.
Dip tortillas in consomé, fill with shredded beef and cheese, and pan-fry until crispy.
Serve with consomé for dipping, topped with cilantro and diced onion.
tips: Use a mix of guajillo and ancho chiles for depth.
The consomé is essential — serve it in small bowls alongside.

name: Cochinita Pibil
desc: Yucatan-style slow-roasted pork marinated in achiote paste and sour orange juice, wrapped in banana leaves.
steps: Blend achiote paste with orange juice, lime juice, and garlic to make the marinade.
Coat pork in marinade, wrap in banana leaves, and refrigerate overnight.
Slow-roast wrapped pork at 150°C for 3 hours until falling apart.
Shred and serve with pickled red onion and habanero salsa.
tips: Seville orange is traditional — substitute with orange plus lime juice.
Pickle red onions in lime juice and salt for at least 30 minutes.

name: Mole Negro
desc: Oaxacan black mole with charred chiles, chocolate, and spices served over chicken.
steps: Char and rehydrate chiles, then blend with toasted sesame seeds, fried plantain, onion, and garlic.
Cook the sauce in oil until darkened, add chocolate and broth, simmer 45 minutes.
Poach chicken thighs in the mole sauce until cooked through.
Serve with rice, garnished with sesame seeds.
tips: Charring the chiles until almost black gives the mole its signature dark color.
This sauce freezes beautifully for months.

name: Mole Verde
desc: Bright green mole made with pumpkin seeds, tomatillos, and herbs, served with pork.
steps: Boil tomatillos and jalapeños until soft, then blend with toasted pumpkin seeds, cilantro, lettuce, and garlic.
Cook the green sauce in oil for 10 minutes until thickened.
Simmer sliced pork in the verde sauce until tender and cooked through.
Serve with warm tortillas and rice.
tips: Toast pumpkin seeds in a dry skillet until they pop for the best flavor.
Lettuce adds body and a mild sweetness to the sauce.

name: Pozole Verde
desc: Green pozole with hominy and chicken in a tangy tomatillo-herb broth.
steps: Simmer chicken in salted water until cooked, shred and reserve the broth.
Roast tomatillos and poblanos, blend with cilantro, onion, and garlic.
Add green sauce and drained hominy to the broth, simmer 30 minutes.
Serve with shredded chicken, radishes, cabbage, and tostadas.
tips: Roasting the tomatillos adds a smoky depth to the green broth.
Garnishes are essential — offer shredded cabbage, radish, oregano, and lime.

name: Tlacoyos
desc: Thick oval masa cakes stuffed with refried beans, topped with nopales, salsa, and cheese.
steps: Knead masa harina with warm water and salt to form a smooth dough.
Flatten into oval shapes, stuff with refried beans, seal and press flat.
Cook on a hot griddle with a little oil until golden on both sides.
Top with diced nopales, salsa verde, crumbled queso fresco, and cilantro.
tips: Keep dough covered with a damp towel to prevent drying.
Nopales can be grilled or boiled — grilling adds better flavor.

name: Enchiladas Suizas
desc: Chicken enchiladas smothered in creamy green tomatillo sauce and melted cheese.
steps: Poach and shred chicken, set aside.
Boil tomatillos and jalapeño, blend with onion, garlic, and sour cream.
Fill tortillas with chicken, roll and place in a baking dish, cover with sauce and cheese.
Bake at 190°C for 20 minutes until bubbly and golden.
tips: Quickly fry tortillas in oil before rolling to prevent them from breaking.
Oaxaca cheese melts beautifully — substitute mozzarella if needed.

name: Enchiladas Rojas
desc: Corn tortillas rolled around cheese and onion, bathed in a rich red chile sauce.
steps: Toast and rehydrate chiles, blend with garlic, onion, oregano, and some soaking liquid.
Strain sauce, fry in oil until darkened and thickened.
Dip tortillas in warm sauce, fill with crumbled cheese and onion, roll up.
Arrange in dish, pour remaining sauce over, top with cheese and sour cream.
tips: Removing chile seeds reduces heat — keep some for spicier enchiladas.
Frying the sauce in oil deepens the flavor significantly.

name: Mexican Ceviche
desc: Fresh raw fish cured in lime juice with tomato, onion, cilantro, and serrano chiles.
steps: Dice fish into small cubes and marinate in lime juice for 25 minutes until opaque.
Dice tomato, onion, cucumber, and serrano chile finely.
Drain excess lime juice from fish, toss with vegetables and cilantro.
Serve with tostadas, topped with sliced avocado.
tips: Use the freshest fish possible — snapper or sea bass work best.
Do not over-cure — fish becomes rubbery after 40 minutes.

name: Aguachile
desc: Raw shrimp marinated in a fiery lime-chile sauce with cucumber and red onion.
steps: Butterfly shrimp and arrange flat on a plate.
Blend lime juice with serrano chiles, cilantro, and salt until smooth.
Pour green chile sauce over shrimp, add sliced cucumber and red onion.
Refrigerate 15 minutes and serve cold with tostadas.
tips: Shrimp should be extremely fresh since they are barely cooked by the acid.
Adjust serrano quantity to your heat tolerance.

name: Gorditas
desc: Thick fried masa pockets stuffed with chicharrón prensado, salsa, and cream.
steps: Mix masa harina with warm water and salt, form thick round patties.
Deep-fry in hot oil until golden and puffed, about 4 minutes per side.
Slice open to create a pocket, stuff with warm chicharrón prensado.
Top with salsa, sour cream, shredded lettuce, and crumbled cheese.
tips: Make the masa patties thick enough to split open after frying.
Can be cooked on a griddle instead of fried for a lighter version.

name: Flautas
desc: Tightly rolled corn tortillas filled with shredded chicken, fried until crispy and golden.
steps: Poach and shred chicken, season with salt and a squeeze of lime.
Place chicken on tortillas, roll very tightly and secure with toothpicks.
Deep-fry in batches until golden and crispy, about 3 minutes.
Serve topped with sour cream, salsa verde, shredded lettuce, and cheese.
tips: Warm tortillas before rolling so they don't crack.
Remove toothpicks before serving.

name: Carnitas Tacos
desc: Slow-braised pork carnitas in small corn tortillas with onion, cilantro, and salsa.
steps: Cut pork into chunks, simmer in water with orange, garlic, and salt until liquid evaporates.
Fry pork in its own rendered fat until edges are crispy and golden.
Warm corn tortillas on a hot griddle.
Assemble tacos with carnitas, diced onion, cilantro, and salsa verde.
tips: The key is letting the liquid evaporate so the pork fries in its own fat.
Use small double-stacked tortillas for authentic street-style tacos.

name: Tacos de Barbacoa
desc: Slow-cooked beef cheeks in a rich chile sauce, served in soft corn tortillas.
steps: Blend rehydrated chiles with garlic, onion, cumin, and vinegar.
Coat beef cheeks in sauce, wrap in foil, and slow-cook at 150°C for 4 hours.
Shred beef and toss with some cooking juices.
Serve in warm tortillas with chopped onion, cilantro, and lime.
tips: Beef cheeks give the most tender result — substitute chuck if unavailable.
Save the cooking juices for a rich consomé.

name: Tacos de Lengua
desc: Tender braised beef tongue sliced and served in corn tortillas with salsa verde.
steps: Simmer beef tongue with onion, garlic, and bay leaves for 3 hours until tender.
Peel the skin off the tongue and dice the meat into small cubes.
Sear diced tongue on a hot griddle until slightly crispy on edges.
Serve in warm tortillas with salsa verde, cilantro, and lime.
tips: The tongue must be fully cooked to peel easily.
Dicing and searing after braising gives the best texture.

name: Tacos de Suadero
desc: Mexico City street tacos with thin-cut beef brisket slow-fried until crispy.
steps: Simmer brisket in salted water until tender, about 1.5 hours, then slice thin.
Fry thin slices in lard on a hot griddle until edges crisp up.
Warm tortillas on the same griddle to absorb some of the meat juices.
Serve with salsa roja, chopped onion, cilantro, and lime.
tips: The meat should be fork-tender before frying.
Use the rendered fat to warm the tortillas for extra flavor.

name: Tacos Dorados
desc: Crispy fried rolled tacos filled with potato, topped with lettuce, cream, and salsa.
steps: Boil and mash potatoes with salt and a pinch of cumin.
Fill tortillas with mashed potato, roll tightly, and secure with toothpicks.
Fry in hot oil until golden and crispy on all sides.
Top with shredded lettuce, sour cream, salsa verde, and crumbled cheese.
tips: Season the potato filling well — it's the star of the dish.
These are also excellent with shredded chicken filling.

name: Enfrijoladas
desc: Tortillas dipped in creamy black bean sauce, filled with cheese and topped with cream.
steps: Blend cooked black beans with onion, garlic, and epazote until smooth.
Cook bean sauce in oil until thickened, adjust consistency with water.
Dip tortillas in warm bean sauce, fold around crumbled cheese.
Serve topped with more sauce, sour cream, sliced onion, and cheese.
tips: Epazote is traditional — substitute cilantro if unavailable.
The sauce should coat the tortillas like a thick blanket.

name: Entomatadas
desc: Tortillas bathed in a simple fresh tomato sauce, filled with cheese.
steps: Roast tomatoes, chile, onion, and garlic, then blend into a smooth sauce.
Fry sauce in oil for 5 minutes until slightly darkened.
Dip tortillas in warm sauce, fold with crumbled cheese inside.
Serve topped with remaining sauce, sour cream, and extra cheese.
tips: Roasting the tomatoes first gives the sauce a deeper, sweeter flavor.
Can add shredded chicken for a heartier version.

name: Papadzules
desc: Yucatecan dish of tortillas filled with hard-boiled eggs in a pumpkin seed sauce.
steps: Toast pumpkin seeds and blend with epazote and warm water into a smooth green sauce.
Hard-boil eggs, peel, and chop.
Dip tortillas in pumpkin seed sauce, fill with chopped egg, and roll.
Top with more sauce and a drizzle of tomato-habanero salsa.
tips: Do not over-blend pumpkin seeds or the oil will separate too early.
The contrast between the green and red sauces makes this dish stunning.

name: Chiles en Nogada
desc: Poblano chiles stuffed with picadillo and topped with walnut cream sauce and pomegranate.
steps: Roast and peel poblanos, make a slit for stuffing.
Cook picadillo with ground pork, diced fruits, and spices.
Blend walnuts with cream cheese and milk for the nogada sauce.
Stuff chiles, cover with walnut sauce, and garnish with pomegranate and parsley.
tips: This is traditionally a room-temperature dish, not served hot.
Best in August-September when pomegranates and fresh walnuts are in season.

name: Rajas con Crema
desc: Roasted poblano strips cooked in a creamy sauce with corn and onions.
steps: Roast and peel poblanos, cut into strips (rajas).
Sauté onion and garlic, add rajas and corn, cook 5 minutes.
Pour in cream, add cheese, and simmer until melted and thickened.
Serve with warm tortillas or as a side dish.
tips: Charring the poblanos well makes peeling easier and adds smokiness.
This is a perfect vegetarian taco filling.

name: Calabacitas con Queso
desc: Mexican sautéed squash with corn, tomatoes, and melted cheese.
steps: Dice squash, tomatoes, onion, and poblano.
Sauté onion and poblano, add squash and corn, cook 10 minutes.
Add diced tomato, season with salt and pepper, cook 5 more minutes.
Top with shredded cheese, cover to melt, and serve.
tips: Mexican gray squash (calabacita) is ideal but zucchini works too.
Don't overcook — the squash should have a slight bite.

name: Ensalada de Nopales
desc: Chilled cactus paddle salad with tomato, onion, cilantro, and queso fresco.
steps: Boil nopales until tender, drain and rinse away the slime, then dice.
Dice tomato and red onion, chop cilantro.
Toss everything with lime juice, oregano, and salt.
Top with crumbled queso fresco and serve at room temperature.
tips: Rinsing cooked nopales well removes the mucilaginous texture.
This salad pairs perfectly with grilled meats.

name: Esquites
desc: Mexican corn in a cup with mayo, lime, chile powder, and cotija cheese.
steps: Sauté corn kernels in butter with epazote until charred and tender.
Toss with mayonnaise and lime juice.
Serve in cups topped with crumbled cotija cheese and chile powder.
Add extra lime wedges on the side.
tips: Use fresh corn cut off the cob for the best flavor and texture.
Epazote is traditional but optional.

name: Picadillo
desc: Mexican ground beef hash with potatoes, tomatoes, and warm spices.
steps: Brown ground beef with onion and garlic, drain excess fat.
Add diced potatoes, chopped tomatoes, cumin, and chipotle.
Simmer covered for 20 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Serve with rice and warm tortillas, garnished with cilantro.
tips: Some regions add raisins and olives for a sweet-savory twist.
This is a perfect filling for empanadas or chiles rellenos.

name: Albóndigas Soup
desc: Mexican meatball soup in a light tomato-chipotle broth with vegetables.
steps: Mix ground beef with cooked rice, egg, and seasonings, form into small meatballs.
Blend tomatoes with chipotle and onion, cook in a pot with broth.
Add meatballs, diced carrots, and zucchini to the broth, simmer 25 minutes.
Serve hot with chopped cilantro and lime wedges.
tips: Adding rice to meatballs keeps them tender and moist.
Do not stir vigorously or meatballs may break apart.

name: Tinga de Pollo
desc: Shredded chicken braised in a smoky chipotle-tomato sauce with onions.
steps: Poach chicken breasts, then shred with two forks.
Blend tomatoes with chipotle, garlic, and oregano.
Sauté sliced onion, add the sauce, simmer 10 minutes.
Add shredded chicken, cook 10 more minutes until flavors meld.
tips: Tinga makes an excellent filling for tostadas, tacos, or tortas.
Adjust chipotle quantity to control the heat and smokiness.

name: Menudo Rojo
desc: Traditional Mexican tripe soup in a red chile broth with hominy.
steps: Clean and boil tripe for 2 hours until tender, cut into small pieces.
Toast and rehydrate chiles, blend with garlic, onion, and oregano.
Add chile sauce and drained hominy to the tripe broth, simmer 30 minutes.
Serve with oregano, diced onion, crushed chile flakes, and lime.
tips: Menudo is traditionally a weekend morning dish, said to cure hangovers.
Thorough cleaning of the tripe is essential.

name: Pambazos
desc: Bread rolls dipped in guajillo sauce and fried, stuffed with potato and chorizo.
steps: Cook crumbled chorizo with diced boiled potatoes until crispy.
Blend rehydrated guajillo chiles with garlic into a smooth sauce.
Dip bread rolls in guajillo sauce, fry on a griddle until crispy outside.
Split rolls, fill with potato-chorizo mixture, top with lettuce, cream, and cheese.
tips: The bread should be completely coated in red sauce before frying.
Pambazo bread is soft and absorbent — use telera or bolillo as substitutes.

name: Torta Ahogada
desc: Guadalajara-style sandwich drowned in a spicy chile de árbol tomato sauce.
steps: Blend roasted tomatoes, chiles de árbol, garlic, and vinegar into a thin spicy sauce.
Split birote rolls, spread with refried beans, fill with warm carnitas.
Place sandwiches in a dish and pour the hot sauce over until drowned.
Serve with sliced onion and lime wedges.
tips: Birote bread is hard-crusted and doesn't dissolve in sauce — use crusty rolls.
This dish is meant to be messy — eat with a fork and knife.

name: Molletes
desc: Open-faced bolillo rolls topped with refried beans, melted cheese, and pico de gallo.
steps: Split bolillos in half, spread generously with refried beans.
Top with shredded cheese and broil until cheese is melted and bubbly.
Make pico de gallo by dicing tomato, onion, jalapeño, and cilantro.
Top molletes with pico de gallo and serve immediately.
tips: Molletes are a classic Mexican breakfast or snack.
Use day-old bread for the best texture when toasted.

name: Sopa de Tortilla
desc: Classic Mexican tortilla soup with fried tortilla strips in a tomato-chile broth.
steps: Cut tortillas into strips and fry until crispy, set aside.
Blend roasted tomatoes, garlic, and onion, fry in oil, add chicken broth.
Toast pasilla chiles and add to simmering soup for 15 minutes.
Ladle into bowls, top with tortilla strips, avocado, cheese, and cream.
tips: The pasilla chile gives a mild, fruity heat — do not substitute with ancho.
Garnishes make this soup — don't skip the avocado and cheese.

name: Sopa de Lima
desc: Yucatecan lime soup with shredded chicken and crispy tortilla strips.
steps: Simmer chicken in broth with onion, garlic, and whole habanero until cooked, then shred.
Roast and dice tomatoes, add to broth with lime juice.
Cut tortillas into strips and fry until crispy.
Serve soup with shredded chicken, tortilla strips, and lime slices.
tips: Use Yucatan limes (lima agria) if available — they have a unique floral flavor.
Keep the habanero whole so it flavors the broth without making it too spicy.

name: Chiles Toreados
desc: Blistered serrano chiles pan-fried with soy sauce and lime, served as a fiery side.
steps: Blister whole serrano chiles in hot oil until charred on all sides.
Add sliced onion rings and cook until softened.
Splash with soy sauce and lime juice, toss well.
Serve immediately as a side dish with any meal.
tips: The soy-lime combination is surprisingly traditional in Mexican taco stands.
Handle with care — the steam from blistering chiles is very potent.

name: Arroz Rojo
desc: Mexican red rice cooked in tomato broth with peas and carrots.
steps: Rinse and soak rice 10 minutes, drain thoroughly.
Fry rice in oil until golden, add blended tomato, onion, and garlic.
Add broth, diced carrot, and peas, bring to a boil.
Cover, reduce to low heat, cook 18 minutes without lifting the lid.
tips: Do not stir the rice once the broth is added or it becomes sticky.
Soaking the rice before frying gives each grain a better texture.

name: Frijoles Charros
desc: Cowboy-style pinto beans stewed with bacon, chorizo, tomato, and chile.
steps: Cook pinto beans until tender (or use canned), reserve the broth.
Fry diced bacon and crumbled chorizo until crispy.
Add diced onion, tomato, jalapeño, and garlic, cook 5 minutes.
Add beans with broth, simmer 20 minutes, finish with cilantro.
tips: These beans are meant to be soupy, not thick.
Add a beer for extra depth of flavor.

name: Frijoles Negros Refritos
desc: Creamy refried black beans with onion and epazote, a staple Mexican side.
steps: Cook black beans until soft, or use canned, reserving liquid.
Fry diced onion and garlic in lard until golden.
Add beans and mash to desired consistency, adding bean liquid as needed.
Cook until thick and creamy, top with crumbled cheese.
tips: Lard gives the most authentic flavor — substitute oil for a lighter version.
Reserve some whole beans to stir in at the end for texture.

name: Empanadas de Pollo
desc: Crispy fried turnovers filled with seasoned shredded chicken and potato.
steps: Make dough from masa harina, water, and salt. Divide into balls and press flat.
Shred poached chicken, mix with diced cooked potato, onion, tomato, and chipotle.
Place filling on dough rounds, fold in half, seal edges with a fork.
Deep-fry until golden and crispy, about 4 minutes per side.
tips: Press dough thin enough so they cook through but thick enough to hold filling.
These can also be baked at 200°C for a lighter version.

name: Mole de Olla
desc: Rustic Mexican beef and vegetable stew in a light ancho-pasilla chile broth.
steps: Simmer beef shank in water until tender, about 1.5 hours, skim broth.
Toast and blend chiles with some broth, strain into the pot.
Add chayote, corn cut in rounds, zucchini, and green beans.
Simmer 20 minutes, add epazote, and serve with lime and tortillas.
tips: This is not the same as thick mole sauces — it should be a light broth.
The vegetables should be cut in large rustic chunks.

name: Camarones al Ajillo
desc: Mexican garlic shrimp sautéed in butter with guajillo chile and lime.
steps: Sauté sliced garlic and crumbled guajillo chile in butter until fragrant.
Add shrimp in a single layer, cook 2 minutes per side until pink.
Deglaze with white wine and lime juice, cook 1 minute more.
Serve immediately with crusty bread, garnished with parsley.
tips: Do not overcook the shrimp — they should be just pink and curled.
Use the biggest shrimp you can find for the best presentation.

name: Camarones a la Diabla
desc: Devilishly spicy shrimp in a fiery red chile sauce.
steps: Toast and rehydrate all chiles, blend with tomato, garlic, and onion.
Fry sauce in oil until darkened and thick, about 8 minutes.
Add cleaned shrimp to the sauce, cook 4 minutes until pink.
Serve with rice and a squeeze of lime.
tips: Remove seeds from chiles de árbol if you want less heat.
This sauce also works beautifully with chicken or fish.

name: Huaraches
desc: Large sandal-shaped masa flatbreads topped with beans, meat, salsa, and cheese.
steps: Mix masa with water and salt, form into large oval sandal shapes.
Press a layer of refried beans inside the masa, seal and flatten.
Cook on a hot griddle until cooked through and slightly crispy.
Top with sliced carne asada, salsa verde, cheese, lettuce, and cream.
tips: The beans inside the masa add flavor and help the huarache hold its shape.
Make sure the griddle is very hot for the best crust.

name: Quesabirria
desc: Birria-style quesadillas with melted cheese and consomé for dipping.
steps: Braise beef in a chile sauce (guajillo, garlic, onion, oregano) for 2 hours until tender.
Shred beef, strain the consomé and reserve.
Dip tortillas in consomé, fill with shredded beef and cheese, fold and griddle until crispy.
Serve with a bowl of hot consomé for dipping, topped with cilantro and onion.
tips: The consomé dip is what makes this dish addictive.
Press the quesadillas firmly on the griddle for maximum crispiness.

name: Tamales de Rajas con Queso
desc: Steamed masa tamales filled with roasted poblano strips and melted cheese.
steps: Soak corn husks in warm water. Roast and peel poblanos, cut into strips.
Beat lard until fluffy, mix in masa, broth, baking powder, and salt until spongy.
Spread masa on husks, add poblano strips and cheese, fold and tie.
Steam tamales upright for 1 hour until masa pulls away from husk.
tips: Test masa readiness: a small ball should float in water.
Let tamales rest 15 minutes after steaming for the masa to set.

name: Tamales de Mole
desc: Traditional tamales filled with shredded chicken and rich mole sauce.
steps: Prepare masa by beating lard, mixing with masa harina, broth, and salt.
Heat mole paste with broth until smooth, toss with shredded chicken.
Spread masa on soaked corn husks, add chicken mole filling, fold.
Steam for 1 hour, garnish with sesame seeds when serving.
tips: Use store-bought mole paste for convenience — Doña María is a good brand.
These freeze well for up to 3 months.

name: Torta de Milanesa
desc: Mexican sandwich with breaded fried cutlet, avocado, beans, and jalapeños.
steps: Pound chicken thin, dredge in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs.
Fry breaded cutlets in hot oil until golden and cooked through.
Split rolls, spread with refried beans, layer with milanesa.
Top with avocado slices, pickled jalapeños, and lettuce.
tips: Pound the chicken very thin and even for quick, crispy frying.
Toasting the bolillo adds a great crunch contrast.

name: Pollo al Pastor
desc: Achiote-marinated chicken grilled with pineapple, pastor-style.
steps: Blend achiote paste with orange juice, garlic, and vinegar to make marinade.
Marinate chicken thighs for at least 1 hour.
Grill chicken and pineapple slices until charred, then dice.
Serve in tortillas with diced pineapple, onion, and cilantro.
tips: Achiote paste gives the signature red-orange color and earthy flavor.
Grilling over charcoal adds the most authentic smoky flavor.

name: Chalupas
desc: Small fried masa boats topped with shredded pork, salsa verde, and onion from Puebla.
steps: Mix masa with water and salt, form small thick rounds and press a rim around edges.
Fry masa boats in hot oil until crispy on the outside.
Top with shredded pork and salsa verde.
Garnish with finely diced raw onion.
tips: Chalupas from Puebla are small — about 8cm across.
The raised rim holds the toppings in place.

name: Tres Leches Cake
desc: Sponge cake soaked in three milks: evaporated, condensed, and heavy cream.
steps: Beat eggs and sugar until pale and fluffy, fold in flour gently.
Bake at 175°C for 25 minutes until golden and springy.
Mix three milks together, poke holes in cake, pour milk mixture over.
Refrigerate overnight, serve with whipped cream and cinnamon.
tips: The cake must be completely cool before adding the milk mixture.
Overnight soaking gives the best results — the cake should be moist but not falling apart.

name: Arroz con Leche
desc: Mexican cinnamon rice pudding made with whole milk, raisins, and condensed milk.
steps: Boil rice in water with cinnamon sticks until tender.
Add whole milk and condensed milk, stir frequently.
Cook on low heat until thick and creamy, about 20 minutes.
Stir in raisins and vanilla, serve warm or chilled.
tips: Stir constantly toward the end to prevent sticking.
Mexican-style is usually creamier than Spanish versions.

name: Flan Napolitano
desc: Creamy Mexican custard with cream cheese on a caramel base.
steps: Caramelize sugar in a flan mold until dark amber.
Blend eggs, condensed milk, evaporated milk, cream cheese, and vanilla.
Pour through a strainer into the caramel-lined mold.
Bake in a water bath at 175°C for 50 minutes, chill overnight and invert.
tips: The water bath prevents the custard from cracking.
Chill at least 8 hours for the caramel to liquefy into a sauce.

name: Horchata
desc: Refreshing Mexican rice water drink with cinnamon and vanilla.
steps: Soak rice and cinnamon sticks in water for at least 4 hours or overnight.
Blend soaked rice and cinnamon with the soaking water until smooth.
Strain through a fine mesh sieve or cheesecloth twice.
Add sugar and vanilla, stir to dissolve, serve over ice.
tips: Double-straining is essential for a smooth, non-gritty drink.
Some vendors add a splash of evaporated milk for richness.

name: Agua de Jamaica
desc: Tart and refreshing hibiscus flower iced tea sweetened with sugar.
steps: Bring water to a boil, add dried hibiscus flowers, remove from heat.
Steep for 15 minutes until deeply red, then strain.
Stir in sugar until dissolved, add lime juice.
Chill and serve over ice.
tips: Adjust sugar to taste — Jamaica is naturally very tart.
The spent flowers can be candied or used in salads.

name: Mexican Hot Chocolate
desc: Rich hot chocolate made with Mexican chocolate, cinnamon, and a hint of chile.
steps: Heat milk with cinnamon stick until simmering.
Add chopped Mexican chocolate and stir until fully melted.
Add vanilla and a pinch of cayenne pepper.
Froth with a molinillo or whisk and serve hot.
tips: Ibarra or Abuelita are the most common Mexican chocolate brands.
Traditional molinillo frothing creates the signature foam.

name: Mango con Chile
desc: Fresh mango slices dusted with chile-lime seasoning, a classic Mexican street snack.
steps: Peel and slice mangoes into spears or cubes.
Squeeze lime juice over mango.
Drizzle with chamoy and dust generously with Tajín.
Serve immediately as a refreshing snack.
tips: Use firm but ripe mangoes — too soft and they fall apart.
Tajín can be substituted with any chile-lime powder.

name: Conchas
desc: Mexican sweet bread rolls topped with a crunchy sugar shell in a seashell pattern.
steps: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, eggs, butter, and milk into a soft enriched dough.
Knead 10 minutes until smooth, let rise 1 hour until doubled.
Make topping: cream butter, sugar, and flour, press shell pattern on top of rolls.
Bake at 180°C for 18 minutes until golden. Cool on rack.
tips: Use a concha cutter for the pattern or score with a knife.
These are best eaten the same day — warm with coffee.

name: Chile Relleno de Queso
desc: Egg-battered poblano chiles stuffed with melted cheese in tomato sauce.
steps: Roast and peel poblanos, make a slit and remove seeds, stuff with cheese.
Separate eggs, beat whites to stiff peaks, fold in yolks and flour.
Coat stuffed chiles in batter, fry until golden on all sides.
Serve in roasted tomato sauce with rice.
tips: The egg batter must be very fluffy — beat whites until stiff peaks form.
Dredge chiles in flour before battering for better adhesion.

name: Coctel de Camarones
desc: Mexican shrimp cocktail in a tomato-citrus broth with avocado and hot sauce.
steps: Mix ketchup, lime juice, orange juice, and hot sauce to make the cocktail base.
Dice cucumber, avocado, and red onion.
Toss shrimp and vegetables with the cocktail sauce.
Serve in tall glasses with saltine crackers and extra hot sauce.
tips: This is nothing like American shrimp cocktail — it is more of a cold soup.
Use the biggest shrimp you can find for the best result.

name: Chamoyada
desc: Frozen mango slush with chamoy sauce and Tajín, a sweet-sour-spicy treat.
steps: Blend frozen mango with water and lime juice until slushy.
Drizzle chamoy inside the cup in a spiral pattern.
Pour mango slush into the cup.
Top with more chamoy and a generous dusting of Tajín.
tips: Use frozen mango straight from the freezer for the best slushy texture.
Rim the cup with chamoy and Tajín for extra flair.

name: Poc Chuc
desc: Yucatecan grilled pork marinated in sour orange and served with pickled onion.
steps: Marinate thin pork slices in orange juice, lime juice, and salt for 30 minutes.
Make xni-pec salsa: dice tomato, habanero, and onion, mix with lime and cilantro.
Grill pork over high heat until charred, about 3 minutes per side.
Serve with xni-pec salsa, pickled red onion, and black beans.
tips: Pound pork very thin for quick, even grilling.
Xni-pec (dog's nose salsa) should be very spicy — the habanero is essential.

name: Chiles Anchos Rellenos
desc: Dried ancho chiles stuffed with a sweet-savory picadillo and topped with walnut cream.
steps: Rehydrate ancho chiles in warm water, carefully remove seeds.
Cook picadillo: brown pork, add diced apple, raisins, piloncillo, and cinnamon.
Stuff chiles with the filling.
Blend walnuts with cream cheese and milk, drizzle over stuffed chiles.
tips: Handle rehydrated chiles gently — they tear easily.
This is a holiday dish, often served for Christmas celebrations.

name: Sincronizadas
desc: Flour tortilla sandwich with ham, cheese, and beans pressed on a griddle.
steps: Spread refried beans on one tortilla, layer ham and shredded cheese.
Top with second tortilla, press together.
Cook on a hot griddle until golden and cheese melts, flip once.
Cut into wedges and serve with avocado slices and salsa.
tips: Press down firmly with a spatula for maximum crispiness.
These are a popular late-night snack in Mexico City.
